# Proof of Theorem 52 (Primitive Element Theorem)

**Theorem 52.** Every field obtained by adjunction of several algebraic numbers over $k$ can also be generated by adjunction of a single algebraic number over $k$.

*Proof.* It suffices to prove the theorem for the adjunction of two numbers. Let $\alpha_1$ have relative degree $n$ over $k$, with conjugates $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_n$, and let $\beta_1$ have relative degree $m$ over $k$, with conjugates $\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_m$. We will show that for a suitable choice of $u, v \in k$, the number

$$\omega_{11} = u\alpha_1 + v\beta_1$$

generates the field $K(\alpha_1, \beta_1; k)$.

Define the $nm$ numbers

$$\omega_{ik} = u\alpha_i + v\beta_k \qquad (i = 1, \ldots, n;\; k = 1, \ldots, m).$$

Choose $u, v \in k$ such that all $\omega_{ik}$ are pairwise distinct. This is possible since only finitely many choices of $u/v$ make two such expressions coincide. Set

$$H(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{k=1}^{m} (x - \omega_{ik}),$$

which is a polynomial over $k$ because its coefficients are symmetric functions in the $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_k$. Now construct the Lagrange interpolation polynomial

$$\Phi(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{m} \beta_k \frac{H(x)}{x - \omega_{ik}}.$$

For each pair $(i,k)$, the factor

$$\frac{H(x)}{x - \omega_{ik}} = \frac{H(x) - H(\omega_{ik})}{x - \omega_{ik}} = \sum_{h=0}^{nm} c_h \frac{x^h - \omega_{ik}^h}{x - \omega_{ik}} = G(x, \omega_{ik})$$

is a polynomial in $x$ and $\omega_{ik}$ with coefficients in $k$, where $G$ denotes a polynomial with coefficients in $k$. Hence

$$\Phi(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{m} \beta_k \, G(x, u\alpha_i + v\beta_k)$$

is a polynomial in $x$ whose coefficients are polynomial expressions in the $\alpha_i$ and $\beta_k$ with coefficients in $k$, and which are formally symmetric in both the $\alpha_i$ and the $\beta_k$. Consequently, these coefficients belong to $k$, so $\Phi(x) \in k[x]$.

Now evaluate at $x = \omega_{11}$. Then $G(\omega_{11}, \omega_{ik})$ vanishes for all $(i,k) \neq (1,1)$, because $\omega_{11} \neq \omega_{ik}$ by construction, while $H(\omega_{ik}) = 0$. Hence

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\Phi(\omega_{11})}{G(\omega_{11}, \omega_{11})}.$$

Thus $\beta_1$ is expressed as a rational function of $\omega_{11}$ with coefficients in $k$. An entirely analogous construction (interchanging the roles of $\alpha$ and $\beta$) shows that $\alpha_1$ is likewise a rational function of $\omega_{11}$.

Therefore $\omega_{11}$ generates the field $K(\alpha_1, \beta_1; k)$, and by induction, any field $K(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_r; k)$ can be generated by a single algebraic number. $\square$
